text: Last class you said: I would like the lamb, and could I have the bill, please.
Today you can say: I have learned a lot, and my confidence has grown.

Last month I started Block 1.
So, Rubens, how long have you studied English with us?
I have studied for six months, and I have improved a lot since Lesson 1.
That is wonderful. What is your strength, and what do you still struggle with?
My strength is speaking, but the past tense is a weakness. I make a few mistakes.
Good reflection. Here is my feedback: review a little every day. What is your next goal?
My goal is to speak at a medical congress with confidence. My progress is steady, and I am ready.
My strength is speaking, and my weakness is the past tense.
Could you give me some feedback, please?
My goal is to speak with more confidence.

Hello again, and welcome to your first review session. Before we continue, let us look back at everything you have done in Block 1. In only seven lessons, you have reactivated your English and built a strong base. You have talked about your daily routine at the clinic, described the past, and shared your life experiences. You have learned to describe people and places, to make plans for a trip, and to order food politely at a restaurant. Your biggest strength is clear: you are not afraid to speak, and your confidence has grown every single week. Of course, you still make a few mistakes with verb tenses, but that is completely normal, and you always learn from them. My advice is simple. Keep your progress steady, review a little every day, and set one small goal for each lesson. You have come a long way, and the best part of the journey is still ahead of you.

When I started Block 1, I could only say a few words in English, and I felt nervous in every single class. Now, after seven lessons, I notice a real difference. I have improved a lot, and I feel more confident when I speak. My favorite lesson was the restaurant, because I learned to order food politely, and I use those phrases when I travel. My biggest goal is to speak with my international colleagues at a medical congress without fear. I know I still have some weaknesses, especially with the past tense, so I review my notes for ten minutes every morning. I also ask my teacher for honest feedback after each class, because good feedback helps me grow. My progress is slow and steady, but it is real. I am proud of how far I have come, and I am ready for the next block.

Here is how I review my progress in English. First, I look back at the lessons I have finished. Then I write down my strengths and my weaknesses. After that, I choose one small goal for the next class. Next, I ask my teacher for feedback and practice the difficult parts. Finally, I review a little every day to keep my progress steady.
